themes.exampleQuote: Bradley, the manager, was great — made it so easy to schedule our upcoming event.
This brewery has a lot of IPAs: that is a positive for me. They let us taste around 5 different beers before we made a decision.
My manager arranged to have my retirement party at Hop Farm — we had a great time in the party room right in front of the open garage door.
It's been QR codes, then table service, and now suddenly you have to order at the bar — with no clear communication.
Absolutely love the lights and the live edge tables. The pool tables were gorgeous old wood.

notable_quote: The key… I WILL BE HERE AGAIN.
sentiment_summary: Reviewers consistently praise the tap list depth, the physical space, and one staff member by name — Bradley gets mentioned in four separate reviews — while a single but pointed voice flags an inconsistent ordering system and a complete lack of floor presence on one visit.
quarter_narrative: Twenty-three Pittsburgh creators posted more than 30 TikToks tagging Hop Farm this quarter, accumulating over 204,000 plays — and Hop Farm itself filmed none of it back, with no TikTok account to show for it. The city is walking through that garage door, ordering a flight, shooting vertical video, and publishing it to audiences Hop Farm has no relationship with and no way to follow up on. On Instagram, the account is genuinely active — 12 posts and 4 Reels in the last 30 days — but engagement sits at 0.47%, suggesting the content is reaching followers who've already checked this place off rather than pulling in new ones. Hidden Harbor leads the Pittsburgh Bars family this issue on social momentum, and the gap between the two is largely a TikTok gap. Closing that distance — not by posting more, but by existing on the platform where the city is already talking about Hop Farm — is the single variable most likely to move this rank before next quarter.
tldr_read: Strong TikTok creator footprint and a clean conversion path, but zero owned presence on the platform doing the heaviest lifting — Ones to Watch, #1 in Pittsburgh Breweries.
tldr_meaning: The trajectory to Icons is less about building an audience from scratch and more about claiming the one that 23 creators have already assembled on Hop Farm's behalf.

playbook.headline: Show up where 204,000 plays already live
playbook.action: 23 creators have posted 30+ TikToks tagging this business this quarter — none of those views flow back to an owned account because one doesn't exist.

playbook.headline: Give the visual catalog a second category
playbook.action: 364 Google photos spread across 7 categories is solid, but the 'By owner' bucket should be driving interior and event-room shots that no customer will think to upload.

playbook.headline: Turn Bradley into a recurring booking signal
playbook.action: Four reviewers named him specifically; a direct private-events link or inquiry form on the Google profile would close the loop between word-of-mouth and actual bookings.
